retrieved_docs.title: General Anesthesia for Surgeons -- Clinical Significance -- Intravenous Anesthetics
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthesia is most commonly achieved via IV sedatives and analgesics induction, followed by volatile anesthetics maintenance.  Patients better tolerate intravenous (IV) induction, but inhalational induction is often used in children or where IV access is problematic.  All IV anesthetics can produce rapid unconsciousness depending on dosage and rate of administration.  Redistribution from the brain to muscle and adipose tissue and metabolism leads to awakening.  Propofol is a phenol agent with rapid onset and a short duration of action and can be used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia.  An induction dose can cause profound respiratory depression. However, Propofol offers the advantage of effortless awakening with minimal residual sedation, even with a prolonged infusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Indications -- Clinical uses of Propofol
retrieved_docs.content: Induction of general anesthesia in patients ≥ three years old, though it may be used as an induction agent if a child less than three years of age has IV access. Maintenance of anesthesia in patients > 2 months old Sedation during monitored anesthesia care for patients undergoing procedures Sedation in intubated, mechanically-ventilated ICU patients

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Gases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetic gases (nitrous oxide, halothane, isoflurane, desflurane, sevoflurane), also known as inhaled anesthetics, are administered as primary therapy for preoperative sedation and adjunctive anesthesia maintenance to intravenous (IV) anesthetic agents (i.e., midazolam, propofol) in the perioperative setting. Inhaled anesthetics enjoy regular use in the clinical setting due to chemical properties that allow the rapid introduction of an agent into arterial blood via the pulmonary circulation compared to the more circuitous route of venous circulation. This activity reviews the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent to members of the interprofessional team for the use of these anesthetic gases.

retrieved_docs.title: Sevoflurane -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical trials have evaluated multiple anesthetic techniques, including volatile, intravenous, and xenon gas anesthetics. Some studies have found no differences in clinical outcomes between volatile and intravenous anesthetics, whereas others have reported biomarker improvements that did not translate into clinical benefits. Meta-analyses have consistently shown reductions in morbidity or mortality with volatile anesthetics compared to total intravenous anesthesia. Xenon gas has also been explored as a cardioprotective agent, but its clinical advantages over other anesthetics remain uncertain. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Sevoflurane -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the theorized mechanism of action of sevoflurane. Describe the adverse effects of sevoflurane. Summarize the pharmacokinetics of sevoflurane. Outline interprofessional team strategies for enhancing care coordination and communication to advance the safe administration of sevoflurane and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sevoflurane -- Administration -- Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Adults MAC Values for Surgical Levels of Anesthesia Age 25 years: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 2.6% Age 40 years: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 2.1% Age 60 years: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 1.7% Age 80 years: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 1.4%

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Gases -- Indications -- FDA-label Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative sedation – primary or adjunctive; rapid induction of sedation, providing amnesia and anesthesia during surgical procedures. Perioperative sedation maintenance – adjunctive; maintains anesthesia after sedation by IV benzodiazepines or propofol.

retrieved_docs.title: Isoflurane -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients: A meta-analysis of 8 studies found that isoflurane significantly reduced emergence agitation in children compared to sevoflurane. No significant differences were found in time to extubation, awakening time, or PACU duration. For children at high risk of emergence agitation, isoflurane should be considered over sevoflurane for maintenance anesthesia. [17] Neonates have a higher metabolic rate and minute alveolar ventilation, leading to increased uptake of volatile anesthetics compared to older patients. The dosage of inhaled anesthetics also depends on the infant's age and level of prematurity. [18] Older patients: The minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of isoflurane decreases with age, so the dose should be adjusted accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative and Anesthesia Awareness -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors Related to Surgical Procedure or Context
retrieved_docs.content: Common to both the emergent trauma and obstetric populations, the potential for difficult airway placement introduces further opportunities for unintended awareness. Intravenous induction agents rapidly redistribute, resulting in lower blood and brain concentrations. At the same time, alveolar delivery of inhaled anesthetics is delayed by definitive airway placement, and necessary replacement with IV agents may be delayed amidst the focus on obtaining airway control. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Inhalation Agents and Their Cardiovascular Effects -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Debate continues over the use of inhaled versus intravenous anesthetic agents during coronary artery bypass graft surgery in patients with coronary artery disease. Studies comparing overall morbidity and mortality outcomes have yielded conflicting results. Recent large meta-analyses suggest that sevoflurane may provide a more favorable cardioprotective effect during cardiac surgery compared to propofol. However, these analyses have not demonstrated differences in morbidity and mortality. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Indications -- Off-Label uses of Propofol
retrieved_docs.content: Status Epilepticus, refractory (children and adults) Treatment of refractory postoperative nausea and vomiting

retrieved_docs.title: Gaseous Anesthetics -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the use, advantages, and disadvantages of sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, and nitrous oxide. Explain the risk and treatment of malignant hyperthermia. Outline the physiologic effects of sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, and nitrous oxide. Summarize the types, advantages, and disadvantages of gaseous anesthetic agents and outline the role of the interprofessional team in providing pain relief using these medications. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Sevoflurane -- Administration -- Pediatric MAC Values for Surgical Levels of Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn to 1-month-old full-term neonates: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 3.3% One to  younger than six months: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 3% Six months to younger than one year: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 2.8% One to younger than three years: Sevoflurane in oxygen: 2.8%

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse group of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. The category “anesthetics, general” includes chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, and propofol as child agents, each representing a distinct chemical class with specific pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic profiles. In addition, xenon—a noble gas—also falls under the classification of general anesthetics, indicating that it shares the core functional purpose of inducing general anesthesia despite its unique mechanism of action. Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of substances that can be employed to achieve controlled, reversible unconsciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a diverse group of agents that are used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical and diagnostic procedures. The class of general anesthetics includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol. Each of these agents has distinct pharmacologic characteristics, such as differences in onset speed, duration of action, potency, and side‑effect profile, which influence their selection for specific clinical scenarios. Understanding the full spectrum of general anesthetic agents—ranging from the historic inhalational agent chloroform to the widely used intravenous agent propofol—allows clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, procedural requirements, and safety considerations, thereby optimizing peri‑operative care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a broad spectrum of agents that are classified under the category “anesthetics, general.” This group includes the inhalational agents chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, and flurothyl, as well as the intravenous agents methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is identified as a specific type of general anesthetic. Together, these substances represent the diverse pharmacologic options used to achieve reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia during surgical and procedural care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Methoxyfluran, a halogenated ether anesthetic, is recognized for its ability to induce فقدان الوعي (loss of consciousness), which is a central therapeutic objective in the administration of general anesthesia. By depressing central nervous system activity, methoxyfluran reliably produces the unconscious state required for surgical procedures, highlighting its role as an effective agent for achieving controlled loss of consciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: OPERATIONS AND ANESTHESIA PROCEDURES is classified as a subcategory of منظار داخلي, indicating that procedures involving surgery and anesthesia are considered part of the broader internal endoscopic (منظار داخلي) domain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2243
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a class of drugs used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical procedures, and they are collectively referred to in Arabic as “التخدير العام.” This category includes a diverse group of agents that are considered “children” of the general anesthetic class, such as chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic, illustrating that both traditional volatile compounds (e.g., chloroform, enflurane, isoflurane) and newer agents (e.g., propofol, methohexital, xenon) belong to the same therapeutic group. Understanding that these substances share the common purpose of producing general anesthesia helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on pharmacologic properties, safety profiles, and specific patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 319
retrieved_docs.content: agent during anesthesia. Together, these terminologies deliver an integrated framework for identifying, classifying, and communicating about diverse pharmacologic and therapeutic substances across patient care and biomedical research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents used to induce reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. Within this class, chloroform, enfluran (enflurane), fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol are recognized as specific child agents that share the overarching function of producing general anesthesia. Xenon is also classified as a type of general anesthetic, reflecting its role as an inhalational agent with anesthetic properties. Although these agents differ in chemical structure, potency, onset and recovery times, and side‑effect profiles, they are all integrated into the therapeutic repertoire for achieving controlled, reversible unconsciousness in patients undergoing operative or diagnostic interventions. Understanding the relationships among these individual anesthetic agents and the general anesthetic category supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies to optimize patient safety and perioperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2045
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetics, general constitute a broad class of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. This class includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol, each of which is a specific child of the general anesthetic category. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic. Together, these diverse compounds—ranging from volatile liquids such as chloroform and isofluran to intravenous agents like propofol and ketamin, and to gaseous agents such as nitrous oxide and xenon—provide clinicians with multiple options for tailoring anesthetic care to the physiological needs, safety considerations, and procedural requirements of individual patients. Understanding the relationships among these agents supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies in peri‑operative medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 394
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulators are characterized as antagonists and inhibitory agents that can block or diminish GABA‑mediated neurotransmission. This pharmacologic profile encompasses a broad spectrum of compounds, including the volatile anesthetic gases chloroform, enflurane, halothane, isoflurane and nitrous oxide; the intravenous anesthetics etomidate, methohexital, propofol and bupivacaine; the barbiturates flurazepam and flurothyl; the dissociative agent fencyclidine; the hypnotic‑sedative chloralose; the carboxylic acids; the inert gas nitrous oxide; the mineral kaolin; as well as amitriptyline, thiorphan and other hypnotic‑sedative agents that are listed as allowed qualifiers for antagonist‑inhibitory actions. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in selecting drugs for anesthesia, sedation, analgesia and neuropsychiatric treatment and in anticipating therapeutic effects, drug interactions and adverse outcomes. Antagonists and inhibitors are frequently qualified by local‑anesthetic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are a pharmacologic class employed to achieve reversible loss of sensation in a specific region of the body. Within this class, mepivakain, prilokain, and propoxykain are identified as child agents of the broader local anesthetic category, while bupivakain and etidokain are also classified as local anesthetics. Recognizing that mepivakain, prilokain, propoxykain, bupivakain, and etidokain share the same fundamental mechanism of action enables clinicians to select appropriate agents for regional blocks, infiltration, or topical anesthesia and to appreciate their related pharmacologic properties, including variations in potency, duration of effect, and safety profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1786
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a diverse group of agents that are classified as child entities within the broader category of general anesthesia. This group includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these compounds possesses distinct pharmacologic characteristics but shares the common purpose of inducing and maintaining a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical and diagnostic procedures. Recognizing that chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný, and propofol are all members of the general anesthetic class helps clinicians select appropriate agents, tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, and optimize peri‑operative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2311
retrieved_docs.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) is identified as the procedural site for neuromuscular blockade (إحصار عصبي عضلي), a technique that targets neural pathways to induce muscle relaxation and is commonly employed in anesthesia and critical care settings.
